nativeRange.habitat: Found in blackwater rivers and seasonally flooded forest (igapó) of the Amazon basin. The Uaru inhabits slow-moving, heavily vegetated reaches with submerged roots and accumulated leaf litter, where the water is soft, acidic, and often tannin-stained.

decorPreferences: large pieces of driftwood and bogwood (tannin-releasing)
smooth rounded rocks for spawning surfaces
leaf litter (dried Indian almond or oak leaves)
minimal or no live plants (Uaru will consume them)
dim lighting supplemented by surface cover or floating plants
open mid-water swimming space for pair bonding

diet.notes: The Uaru is among the most strictly herbivorous of the large South American cichlids — an unusual trait for a cichlid of its size. The staple diet should be plant-based: spirulina wafers, algae flakes, blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini, cucumber, peas, romaine lettuce), and high-quality vegetable-based cichlid pellets. Fresh or frozen plant matter is enthusiastically consumed; live aquatic plants will be destroyed. Occasional protein supplementation (blanched earthworms, high-quality cichlid pellets with moderate protein) is acceptable, but the diet must remain predominantly vegetable matter. A high-fibre, low-protein feeding regime is important — excessive protein is strongly associated with intestinal bloat and HITH (hole-in-the-head disease). In the wild, fish browse algae, fallen fruit, and aquatic vegetation in flooded forest.

goodWith: discus (Symphysodon spp.) — share identical water requirements
large peaceful tetras (e.g., cardinal tetras in large groups)
other large peaceful Amazonian cichlids of similar size
bristlenose plecos and other armoured catfish
eartheater cichlids (Geophagus, Satanoperca) of similar size
avoidWith: aggressive or territorial cichlids (Oscars, Dovii, Midas cichlids)
small fish that may be consumed or stressed
bottom-dwelling catfish that compete for territory during spawning

imageCandidates.notes: Herbisius; 800x600. Sharp full-body live adult in aquarium showing the canonical grey-blue coloration, bold black flank triangle, and red-ringed eye - the key diagnostic features.
Brent Tibbats; 640x443. Sharp live adult with vivid blue head, orange eye, and strong black flank marking; lower resolution so kept as a secondary vibrant angle.

summary: The Uaru, also called the Triangle Cichlid, is a large, peaceful Amazonian cichlid and one of the very few large South American cichlids that feeds primarily on plant matter. Juveniles are cryptic brown with pale spots; adults develop a grey-blue body marked by a bold black triangle. Bonded pairs raise fry on parental skin secretions, as discus do. Suitable for experienced keepers.
careNotes: Uaru are among the most rewarding large Amazonian cichlids for experienced keepers, but their requirements are exacting. A mated pair demands a minimum 450 L (ideally 600 L+) aquarium with a footprint of at least 150 cm to allow natural pair-bonding behaviour and territorial buffer from tankmates. Water must replicate Amazon blackwater: warm (26–30°C), soft (gH 1–8), and acidic (pH 5.0–7.0); hard or alkaline conditions lead to chronic stress, immune suppression, and hole-in-the-head disease. Tannin-stained water from driftwood and Indian almond leaves is strongly recommended. Diet is the most critical and commonly mismanaged aspect of Uaru care: they are strict herbivores, and a diet high in animal protein will rapidly cause intestinal bloat. The bulk of feeding (80%+) must be plant-based — spirulina wafers, blanched vegetables, algae-rich flakes and pellets. Live plants will be consumed enthusiastically; the aquascape must rely on driftwood, smooth stones, and leaf litter rather than planted vegetation. Uaru are peaceful for their size and do well with discus, large tetras, and armoured catfishes sharing the same blackwater biotope. Pairs should not be separated once bonded. Juveniles display a cryptic brown-and-white spotted pattern and can be sold as a different fish; the adult grey-blue coloration with the distinctive black flank triangle develops progressively from around 6–8 cm. A notable and rare reproductive adaptation is seen in this species: like discus, both parents secrete a nutritious mucus from their skin on which newly hatched fry feed for the first weeks of life.
breedingNotes: Uaru are substrate spawners that form strong, life-long monogamous pair bonds. Conditioning on a high-quality vegetable diet and maintaining very soft, warm, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, 28–30°C) is essential to trigger spawning. Pairs clean a flat stone or broad piece of driftwood and deposit 200–800 pale amber eggs. Both parents guard the spawn vigorously. Fry hatch in approximately 48–60 hours and are moved by parents to shallow pits in the substrate. A defining feature of Uaru reproduction — shared only with discus among aquarium fish — is biparental skin-mucus feeding: for the first 10–14 days, fry feed by grazing the nutritious mucus secreted by both parents’ skin, gradually transitioning to infusoria, baby brine shrimp, and finely powdered spirulina. Separating fry too early dramatically reduces survival. Breeding difficulty is rated advanced due to demanding water parameters, the need for an established bonded pair, large tank footprint, and the intensive fry-rearing period.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Uaru amphiacanthoides
primary.notes: Primary source. Detailed husbandry including water parameters (pH 5.0–6.5, temperature 26–30°C, hardness 18–179 µS/cm), adult size (to 30 cm), native range (Amazon basin), diet (herbivore, vegetable matter), breeding (substrate spawner, monogamous pair, skin-mucus fry feeding), and compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Uaru amphiacanthoides
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult size (to 30 cm TL), native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins), diet (herbivore, primarily algae and plant matter), and temperature range (24–30°C). Confirmed monotypic genus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Uaru Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-focused care guide confirming herbivore diet requirements, tank size recommendation (125+ gallons for a pair), blackwater parameters, peaceful temperament, discus-compatible tankmates, and juvenile colour phase description (brown-spotted juveniles vs. grey-blue adults).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Uaru amphiacanthoides species profile
additional.notes: Species profile confirming Amazon blackwater biotope, adult size 25–30 cm, advanced care level, strict herbivore diet, monogamous pair bonding, skin-mucus fry feeding behaviour, and the distinctive juvenile-to-adult colour transition (triangle marking).

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum.com — Uaru amphiacanthoides species article
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid community source. Confirmed breeding notes (egg count 200–800, parental care, fry mucus feeding period 10–14 days), conspecific aggression level, diet strictly herbivorous, soft acidic blackwater requirements, and minimum tank dimensions for a breeding pair.

fish.breedingNotes: Uaru are substrate spawners that form permanent monogamous pair bonds. Spawning is encouraged by maintaining very soft, acidic, warm water (pH 5.5–6.5, 28–30°C) and conditioning the pair on a high-quality vegetable diet. A large flat spawning surface — a smooth stone or broad piece of driftwood — is required. Both parents actively guard eggs and larvae. For the first one to two weeks after hatching, fry feed on nutritious mucus secreted from both parents' skin, a behaviour shared with discus among commonly kept cichlids. Fry must not be separated from their parents prematurely. A typical spawn yields 200–800 eggs, which hatch in approximately 48–60 hours.
